title: Mickey 17
shortDescription: Bong Joon-ho's next big-scale sci-fi swing lands early in the year.
editorialBlurb: A buzzy auteur project with mainstream curiosity, award chatter potential, and the kind of premise people will explain to each other all weekend.

title: Project Hail Mary
shortDescription: A high-concept survival story positioned as a broad audience sci-fi event.
editorialBlurb: This has big mainstream crossover potential if the adaptation lands, making it exactly the sort of release people will want contextualized in a clean calendar.

title: Euphoria
shortDescription: A long-gap prestige return with a built-in social media aftershock.
editorialBlurb: Even without an exact date, this belongs on the radar because the return window alone will create cultural noise and catch-up viewing.

title: The Mandalorian and Grogu
shortDescription: Star Wars returns to theaters with one of its safest franchise bets.
editorialBlurb: The combination of brand recognition, big-screen scale, and fan familiarity makes this an obvious cultural-event entry on the calendar.

title: Daredevil: Born Again
shortDescription: Marvel's street-level corner keeps its momentum with another season.
editorialBlurb: For fans who care about franchise continuity but do not want to sift through every Marvel announcement, this is exactly the level of significance the product should surface.

title: Masters of the Universe
shortDescription: A familiar IP relaunch with curiosity value well beyond core fandom.
editorialBlurb: This sits in the sweet spot for Release Radar: recognizable enough to matter, uncertain enough that people will want a quick editorial read before committing attention.

title: Ted Lasso
shortDescription: A feel-good comeback with broad household recognition.
editorialBlurb: Even if the final shape changes, the return of a comfort-watch brand deserves to be visible because it cuts across casual and heavy TV audiences.

title: Toy Story 5
shortDescription: A legacy Pixar franchise returns with automatic multigenerational interest.
editorialBlurb: This is the kind of title people plan around early, making it one of the clearest examples of a release that earns a top-tier heat score.

title: Supergirl: Woman of Tomorrow
shortDescription: The DC reboot effort widens with a title that signals tone and ambition.
editorialBlurb: Whether people are all-in or skeptical, they will still want the release anchored on the calendar because it is part of a larger franchise reset.

title: The Sandman
shortDescription: A cult-favorite fantasy adaptation enters the finale conversation.
editorialBlurb: This is a useful mid-tier inclusion: meaningful enough for the audience that follows prestige streaming, but not so obvious that every app will highlight it well.

title: Moana
shortDescription: Disney revisits a recent animated favorite for a broad family rollout.
editorialBlurb: Brand familiarity alone makes this highly visible, and the adaptation angle gives it enough curiosity to warrant a prominent slot.

title: The Odyssey
shortDescription: A prestige epic with blockbuster scale and instant conversation value.
editorialBlurb: This is exactly the kind of release the app should frame as an event: a title with awards weight, social chatter, and broad curiosity beyond core cinephiles.

title: House of the Dragon
shortDescription: Prestige franchise TV with a predictable online discourse spike.
editorialBlurb: Even before a precise premiere date arrives, audiences want to know the likely season window because it anchors a chunk of the cultural TV conversation.

title: Spider-Man: Brand New Day
shortDescription: A marquee superhero title built for opening-weekend conversation.
editorialBlurb: This belongs at the very top of the year because the interest floor is high before marketing even fully arrives.

title: The Bear
shortDescription: An awards-friendly critical darling that still drives mainstream interest.
editorialBlurb: This is the kind of show that benefits from editorial curation because casual viewers want to know whether the next season is a genuine event or just another drop.

title: A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms
shortDescription: Another Game of Thrones universe entry with strong fandom carryover.
editorialBlurb: Spin-off fatigue is real, but franchise gravity still makes this culturally relevant enough to track as a notable premiere window.

title: The Last of Us
shortDescription: One of television's biggest prestige genre brands stays on the board.
editorialBlurb: When a show can dominate both awards talk and mainstream group-chat chatter, it deserves a top-tier position even before the exact date locks.

title: The Batman Part II
shortDescription: A sequel that sits at the intersection of franchise scale and auteur credibility.
editorialBlurb: The overlap between comic-book fandom and prestige-minded moviegoers makes this one of the cleanest examples of a five-flame event.

title: The Devil Wears Prada 2
shortDescription: A legacy sequel with outsized attention from well beyond franchise circles.
editorialBlurb: This is the sort of release traditional databases treat like any other entry, but it clearly deserves extra signal because of its meme and nostalgia potential.

title: Black Phone 2
shortDescription: A genre sequel timed for peak spooky-season interest.
editorialBlurb: Not everything needs to be a franchise mega-event to matter; this is a strong example of a release that will overperform in conversation within its genre lane.

title: Severance
shortDescription: A premium streaming obsession with heavy theory-discourse energy.
editorialBlurb: This is a textbook Release Radar title because the audience wants both the date and a quick signal that this season is culturally hotter than a routine platform drop.

title: Presumed Innocent
shortDescription: A prestige streamer follow-up with enough crossover appeal to watch closely.
editorialBlurb: This is the kind of release that can get lost in bigger apps but fits neatly inside a curated product trying to separate signal from platform volume.

title: Ahsoka
shortDescription: A Disney+ franchise continuation with clear fandom gravity.
editorialBlurb: This is an easy inclusion because it has enough built-in attention to matter, but still benefits from editorial context around where it sits in the larger franchise calendar.

title: The White Lotus
shortDescription: A cast-announcement machine that reliably becomes appointment TV.
editorialBlurb: This is a perfect culture-calendar title because each season quickly turns into a weekly social event, not just another prestige release.

title: The Studio
shortDescription: An industry satire with enough insider heat to break outward.
editorialBlurb: Not every selection needs blockbuster scale. This belongs because it appeals to the exact audience that wants curated signal rather than exhaustive volume.

title: The Hunger Games: Sunrise on the Reaping
shortDescription: A franchise prequel with built-in YA nostalgia and broad awareness.
editorialBlurb: This deserves a lead-card position because audiences outside the core fandom will still know the brand and want to know when it returns.

title: Frozen III
shortDescription: A holiday corridor giant with almost no ceiling on awareness.
editorialBlurb: This is one of the clearest annual peak titles because it matters to families, casual moviegoers, and anyone tracking the broader culture calendar.

title: Avengers: Doomsday
shortDescription: A flagship Marvel event built to dominate year-end conversation.
editorialBlurb: Whether enthusiasm is soaring or skeptical, this is absolutely the type of release the product exists to spotlight with strong editorial framing.

title: Shrek 5
shortDescription: A nostalgia-heavy holiday launch with broad meme and family appeal.
editorialBlurb: This is the kind of release databases flatten into noise, but a curated app should elevate because the cultural footprint is obvious well before opening weekend.
